text: The surgeon begins the dissection at the right crura of the diaphragm, initiating the surgical procedure.
The surgeon identifies the avascular plane and uses a chopstick technique to manipulate the hernia sac anteriorly.
The surgeon continues the dissection using a vessel sealer, focusing on separating tissue planes during the hiatal hernia repair with Nissen fundoplication.
The surgeon dissects the short gastric vessels to connect with the previously completed anterior dissection, facilitating the continuation of the hiatal hernia repair and Nissen fundoplication.
The surgeon begins the posterior dissection on the left side, progressing towards the right side where the previous dissection was completed, and then seamlessly connects the two dissection planes.
The surgeon positions a mesh over the surgical site, ensuring it extends approximately four centimeters.
The surgeon continues the posterior dissection to facilitate a tension-free wrap, ensuring the anatomical structures are properly prepared for the Nissen fundoplication.
The surgeon uses a chopstick technique to gain additional length in the tissue, manipulating the structures to facilitate the surgical procedure.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to control bleeding and manage tissue during the procedure.
The surgeon repairs the Prus using an Oethaban suture with pledgets, securing the tissue in place.
The surgeon applies the Slipknot technique to control tension during the procedure, ensuring precise manipulation of the surgical site.
The surgeon chops down on the sutures, lifting the needle side while pulling down on the opposite side to remove it from the tissue, then converts the square knot into a slip knot.
The surgeon creates an air knot by alternating hand movements, lifting up on the suture to form the knot above the tissue surface.
The surgeon completes a four-centimeter posterior dissection, creating space for the anatomical structure to be positioned appropriately.
The surgeon places tissue in the area where the surgical mesh will be positioned, preparing the site for mesh placement.
The surgeon places the mesh onto the surgical site, ensuring the 'R' marking is oriented correctly so that the ST side faces the abdomen.
The surgeon places tissue over the top of the mesh and proceeds to perform the wrap, likely part of the Nissen fundoplication procedure.
The surgeon pulls the wrap through, ensuring it is tension-free, and performs a shoe-shine maneuver to finalize the placement.
